planting.note: Plant a young tree in spring after the ground has thawed, in deep moist soil with room to grow large.

directions: Dawn redwood is a fast-growing deciduous conifer often called a living fossil, prized for feathery fern-like foliage that turns coppery orange before dropping in fall. Give it full sun and consistently moist, well-drained soil; it tolerates wet sites and even seasonal flooding better than most conifers. Choose a location with abundant vertical and horizontal space, as mature trees reach 70 to 100 feet with a broad conical form. Water deeply during establishment and through dry spells for the first few seasons. It needs no pruning to develop its naturally elegant shape.

planting.note: Plant tubers after all danger of frost has passed and soil has warmed.

directions: Day Dreamer is a waterlily-form dahlia bearing soft blooms blending apricot, pink, and buttercream on productive plants. Plant tubers horizontally about 4 to 6 inches deep once soil warms, positioning the eye upward, and hold off watering until sprouts emerge to prevent rot. Pinch the growing tip when plants reach about a foot to encourage bushier growth and more blooms. Stake or corral tall stems and cut flowers regularly to keep the plant producing. In zones colder than 8, lift and store the tubers in a cool, dry place over winter.

planting.note: Plant divisions or potted plants in spring in rich, moist soil.

directions: Deutschland astilbe is a shade-loving perennial that sends up feathery white plumes above glossy green foliage in early summer. Plant it in partial to full shade with consistently moist, humus-rich soil, as it dislikes drying out. Work compost into the planting hole and mulch to conserve moisture around its shallow roots. Divide clumps every three to four years in spring to maintain vigor. Deadheading is optional; the dried plumes add winter interest, and the flowers attract pollinators.

planting.note: Plant tubers after frost danger passes and soil warms.

directions: Diva is a tall, prolific dahlia producing rich plum blooms with exceptional vase life for a large flower. Set tubers about 4 to 6 inches deep in full sun once soil is warm, and wait to water heavily until growth appears. Because plants grow very tall, install sturdy stakes or a support cage early in the season. Cut or deadhead spent flowers frequently to prolong blooming into fall. Lift and store tubers over winter where the ground freezes.

planting.note: Plant in spring with the crown a couple inches below the soil surface.

directions: Dr. Ruppel is a vigorous large-flowered clematis with pink, star-shaped blooms accented by a bold magenta bar down each petal. Give it a sunny position for the vines but keep the roots cool and shaded with mulch or low companion plantings. Provide a trellis, arbor, or fence for the climbing stems to grip, and tie in new growth as needed. This variety blooms on both old and new wood, so prune only lightly in late winter to shape and remove dead stems. Water regularly and feed in spring for the best flower show.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings after frost danger passes; overwinter indoors in cold climates.

directions: Dragon fruit is a climbing, white-fleshed cactus that produces dramatic sculptural stems and sweet fruit following its large night-blooming flowers. Grow it in fast-draining cactus soil and provide a sturdy trellis or post for the heavy vining stems to climb. Water only when the soil dries out, since the plant stores moisture and is prone to rot if kept wet. It needs warmth above 40 degrees, so grow it in a container to move indoors where frost occurs. Hand-pollinate the evening flowers for reliable fruit set.

planting.note: Plant in fall or early spring in acidic, well-drained soil.

directions: Dream Weaver is a sasanqua camellia bearing abundant double blooms of deep pink to lavender edged in white, flowering from fall into winter. Plant it in partial shade in moist, acidic, well-drained soil enriched with organic matter, and mulch to keep roots cool. Protect from harsh afternoon sun and drying winds, especially in warmer zones. Prune lightly right after flowering to shape the evergreen form. Feed with an acid-loving-plant fertilizer in spring for lush foliage and reliable bloom.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist soil with room for a large shade tree.

directions: Dura Heat is a river birch selection with creamy exfoliating bark and strong tolerance of summer heat and humidity. Plant it in full sun to part shade in moist, slightly acidic soil; it thrives in wet spots where other trees struggle. Water deeply during establishment and dry spells to prevent leaf drop. Its resistance to bronze birch borer makes it a durable landscape and shade tree. Prune only in summer or fall, since birches bleed heavily if cut in late winter or early spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil.

directions: Dwarf Alberta spruce is a slow-growing conifer forming a naturally dense, cone-shaped pyramid of soft green needles, ideal for small gardens and containers. Plant it in full sun and well-drained soil, avoiding hot, reflective spots that can scorch the fine foliage. Provide good air circulation to reduce spider mite pressure, and rinse the foliage periodically in dry weather. Water consistently, as its shallow roots dry out quickly in pots. It rarely needs pruning thanks to its tidy, formal habit.

planting.note: Plant after frost danger; grow in a container to overwinter indoors in cold climates.

directions: Dwarf Cavendish is a compact banana cultivar that fruits at a manageable height, making it well suited to containers and small spaces. Grow it in full sun in rich, well-drained soil, feeding heavily through the warm season since bananas are gross feeders. Keep the soil consistently moist and shelter the large leaves from strong wind that can shred them. Where temperatures drop below the mid-50s, move the plant indoors to a bright, warm spot for winter. Each stem fruits once, after which it is cut down and replaced by new pups from the base.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil.

directions: Dwarf Hinoki cypress is a slow-growing evergreen with rich green, fan-shaped sprays of foliage that form a graceful, sculptural pyramid. Plant it in full sun to light shade in moist but well-drained soil, avoiding soggy sites that cause root rot. It appreciates shelter from harsh drying winter winds, which can brown the foliage. Water regularly during establishment and in droughts, then it becomes fairly self-sufficient. Little pruning is needed, and its naturally elegant layered form makes it a favorite for rock gardens and containers.

planting.note: Plant in spring in well-drained soil; provide room for a small ornamental tree.

directions: Eastern redbud is a small deciduous tree famed for the rosy-pink blossoms that clothe its bare branches in early spring before the heart-shaped leaves emerge. Plant it in full sun to partial shade in moist, well-drained soil; it naturally grows as an understory tree and tolerates some shade. Water regularly during the first couple of years to establish its somewhat sensitive root system. Prune only to remove dead or crossing branches after flowering, since it blooms on old wood. Its early nectar is valuable to bees and other pollinators.

planting.note: Plant while dormant in early spring in full sun.

directions: Elberta, sometimes called Early Elberta, is an early-fruiting freestone peach with juicy, flavorful yellow flesh and good disease resistance. Plant it in full sun in deep, well-drained soil, avoiding frost pockets that can damage early blossoms. This variety is self-fruitful, so a single tree will set a crop without a pollinator. Prune annually in late winter to an open-center shape to boost light penetration and fruit quality, and thin young fruit to improve size. Water deeply and consistently while fruit is developing.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil, spacing for a hedge if desired.

directions: Emerald Green arborvitae is a narrow, upright evergreen with dense sprays of bright green foliage, widely planted in rows for privacy screens and hedges. Plant in full sun to light shade in moist, well-drained soil, spacing plants roughly 2 to 4 feet apart for a solid living wall. Water regularly during the first two seasons to establish deep roots and prevent winter browning. It holds its color and slender form well without shearing, though it can be trimmed lightly. Protect young plants from heavy snow load and browsing deer.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in rich, moist soil with afternoon shade.

directions: Endless Summer is a reblooming bigleaf hydrangea that flowers on both old and new wood, producing large mophead blooms from spring through fall. Plant it in morning sun with afternoon shade in rich, consistently moist soil, and mulch to keep roots cool. Flower color shifts with soil chemistry: acidic soil yields blue blooms while alkaline soil produces pink, so amend accordingly. Because it blooms on new growth too, light pruning is forgiving, though it rebounds best with minimal cutting. Keep it well watered, as wilting in heat is common.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in acidic, well-drained soil with afternoon shade.

directions: Ever After Ruby is a reblooming azalea that produces vivid ruby-red flowers in spring and again from summer into fall. Plant it in dappled or partial shade in acidic, well-drained soil rich in organic matter, and mulch to protect the shallow roots. Keep the soil evenly moist but never waterlogged, since azaleas resent both drought and soggy feet. Shear lightly right after the spring flush to encourage a fuller shape and stronger repeat bloom. Feed with an acid-loving-plant fertilizer in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun with well-drained soil.

directions: This everbearing mulberry yields sweet, plump berries over a long season on a compact, shrubby tree that is easy to manage. Plant it in full sun in well-drained soil; it tolerates a range of conditions and is quite drought resistant once established. Prune in winter to keep it at a pickable height and to open the canopy. The fruit ripens over many weeks, so harvest regularly and expect birds to compete for the crop. Note that ripe berries stain, so site it away from patios and walkways.

planting.note: Plant in spring in well-drained soil; excellent drainage is essential.

directions: Evolution Emerald Ice is a low, drought-tolerant sedum grown as a groundcover, offering fresh green succulent foliage and pink summer blooms that draw pollinators. Plant it in full sun in gritty, sharply drained soil, since wet or heavy ground causes rot. Once established it thrives on neglect and needs little supplemental water. Shear back lightly after flowering to keep the mat dense and tidy. Divide clumps in spring every few years to expand your planting or renew vigor.

planting.note: Plant after frost danger; grow in a pot to overwinter indoors in cold zones.

directions: Fignomenal is a true dwarf fig reaching only 2 to 3 feet, making it ideal for containers and small patios while still producing full-sized sweet fruit. Grow it in full sun in well-drained soil and water consistently, easing off as fruit ripens to concentrate flavor. Its compact size makes it easy to move indoors to a cool bright space for winter in colder regions. Prune lightly in late winter to shape and remove weak wood. Feed during the growing season for steady production of its self-fertile crop.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil.

directions: Firefly is a compact bush honeysuckle prized for its glowing amber-to-gold foliage and small yellow flowers borne over a long season. Adaptable and tough, it grows in full sun to part shade and tolerates poor, dry, or rocky soils once established. Its dense habit and spreading roots make it useful for erosion control on slopes and banks. Prune in late winter or early spring to renew and control size, since it blooms on new wood. The nectar-rich flowers attract bees and other pollinators.

planting.note: Plant while dormant in early spring on a sturdy trellis in full sun.

directions: Flame Red Seedless is a vigorous grape producing sweet, juicy, ruby-red seedless berries in large clusters. Plant it in full sun in well-drained soil and provide a strong trellis or arbor to support the heavy vining growth. Train a permanent trunk and cordons the first years, then prune hard every dormant season to a few productive spurs or canes, as grapes fruit on the current year's growth. Water steadily while fruit swells but reduce moisture as harvest approaches for sweeter grapes. Good air circulation helps prevent mildew.

planting.note: Plant in spring in well-drained soil with room for a small tree.

directions: Flame Thrower is a redbud selection celebrated for multicolored foliage that emerges burgundy and matures through orange and yellow to green, plus rosy-red spring flowers on bare branches. Plant it in full sun to partial shade in moist, well-drained soil, giving the best color in a spot with some sun. Water regularly during the first couple of seasons to establish its roots. Prune lightly after flowering only to shape or remove dead wood. The early blooms are a valuable nectar source for pollinators.

planting.note: Plant while dormant in early spring in full sun.

directions: Flavortop is a fast-growing nectarine producing large, juicy, well-balanced freestone fruit that ripens in mid to late summer. Plant it in full sun in deep, well-drained soil, avoiding low frost pockets that harm early bloom. It is self-fruitful, so one tree will bear without a partner. Prune yearly in late winter to an open-center form for light and airflow, and thin developing fruit to boost size and prevent limb breakage. Provide steady water while fruit develops, and manage peach leaf curl with dormant sprays if needed.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, shady soil.

directions: Francee is a classic shade hosta with dark green leaves crisply edged in white, forming a tidy mounded clump and sending up lavender flower spikes in summer. Plant it in partial to full shade in rich, moist, well-drained soil, as too much sun scorches the variegated foliage. Keep the soil evenly moist and mulch to retain moisture and suppress weeds. Divide clumps in spring every few years to propagate or reduce crowding. Watch for slugs, which can chew the leaves, especially in damp conditions.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in rich, moist soil with afternoon shade.

directions: Frill Ride is a bigleaf hydrangea bearing bold mophead blooms with distinctive frilled petal edges in shades that shift with soil pH. Plant it in morning sun and afternoon shade in rich, consistently moist, well-drained soil, mulching to keep roots cool. Acidic soil pushes blooms toward blue while alkaline soil yields pink, so amend to steer the color you want. Prune just after flowering, since it blooms mainly on old wood. Water generously in heat to prevent the large leaves and flowers from wilting.

planting.note: Plant in spring in acidic, well-drained soil in a sheltered spot.

directions: Frost Proof is a cold-tolerant gardenia bearing intensely fragrant white flowers against glossy evergreen leaves, and it withstands cooler temperatures better than typical gardenias. Plant it in morning sun with afternoon shade in acidic, well-drained soil rich in organic matter, and mulch to keep roots cool and moist. Keep watering consistent, as fluctuating moisture causes bud drop. Feed with an acid-loving-plant fertilizer during the growing season and prune lightly after the main bloom. Protect from harsh winter wind at the colder edge of its range.

planting.note: Plant while dormant in early spring in full sun.

directions: Frost is a hardy peach bred for cooler climates, offering sweet, juicy fruit and notable resistance to peach leaf curl. Plant it in full sun in deep, well-drained soil, and it tolerates spring frosts better than many varieties. It is self-fruitful, producing a crop from a single tree. Prune annually in late winter to an open-center shape and thin the young fruit to improve size. Water deeply during fruit development for the best harvest.

planting.note: Plant while dormant in early spring in full sun.

directions: Fuji is a Japanese-bred apple beloved for its exceptionally sweet flavor and crisp, dense texture that stores well. Plant it in full sun in well-drained soil, and provide a compatible pollinating apple nearby since Fuji is not self-fertile. Prune each dormant season to a central-leader or open form for light and air, and thin fruit clusters to improve size. Water consistently during establishment and dry spells. Fuji ripens late in the season, so allow ample time on the tree for full sweetness.

planting.note: Plant after frost danger; grow in a container to move indoors in cold climates.

directions: Fukushu is a compact kumquat producing small oval citrus with an edible sweet rind and tart flesh, well suited to container growing. Grow it in full sun in rich, well-drained soil and water when the top inch dries, avoiding both drought stress and soggy roots. In climates with frost, keep it potted so it can move to a bright indoor spot for winter. Feed regularly with a citrus fertilizer during active growth. It is self-fertile, so a single tree will set fruit.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun with well-drained soil.

directions: Fuyu is a non-astringent persimmon whose sweet, firm fruit can be eaten crisp like an apple while still firm. Plant it in full sun in well-drained soil; it is self-fruitful and needs no pollinator. Water regularly during establishment and while fruit develops, then it tolerates some drought. Prune in dormancy to build a strong framework, since heavy fruit can break branches. The fruit ripens in fall and holds on the tree well after the leaves drop, creating an ornamental display.

planting.note: Plant while dormant in early spring in full sun.

directions: Gala is a widely grown apple with a sweet, mild flavor and firm, crisp flesh that suits fresh eating and cooking alike. Plant it in full sun in well-drained soil and include a compatible pollinating apple nearby, as Gala benefits from cross-pollination. Prune every dormant season for structure and light, and thin fruit clusters to improve size and prevent limb breakage. Water steadily during establishment and dry periods. Gala ripens in late summer to early fall, earlier than many other apples.
